title: A protocol to enumerate total and viable bacterial cells using QUANTOM Tx ™ Microbial Cell Counter
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"wtjoq","text":"Accurate quantification of viable and non-viable bacteria is fundamental to most microbiological studies, thus, rapid and simple techniques are required for this purpose. The current protocol describes a procedure using the QUANTOM Tx™ Microbial Cell Counter (Logos Biosciences Ltd., Korea), a novel instrument for the quantitative measurement of total and viable microbial cells in a solution, using several bacterial species including Listeria monocytogenes, Escherichia coli, Campylobacter jejuni and Brucella species.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":437,"length":22},{"style":"italic","offset":461,"length":16},{"style":"italic","offset":479,"length":20},{"style":"italic","offset":504,"length":8}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"rajsv","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"kcdwk","text":"Introduction","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":12}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"aargo","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9ttuu","text":"Accurate quantification of bacteria, both live and dead, is crucial in many fields of microbiological research, biological services, industrial fermentation processes and diagnostic applications. Traditionally, the bacterial culture method using agar plate count is widely used for the enumeration of live bacteria.  The agar plate counting allows the enumeration of live bacterial concentrations by counts of colony-forming units (CFU) based on the assumption that each CFU grows from one bacterium. However, if bacterial aggregates are not segregated to single cells and the cells are not evenly dispersed on the plate, it leads to the likelihood that each CFU may arise from more than one bacterium, and the resulting CFU count will underestimate the actual number of viable bacteria present (Jansson and Prosser 1997). The total viable cell population may, however, contain all or a subpopulation in a \"viable but nonculturable\" (VBNC) state (Jansson and Prosser 1997).  The fact that the bacteria in a VBNC state are still able to cause infection highlights the importance of alternatives to the culture-based methods for monitoring. Furthermore, bacteria that are injured or starved can not be counted by direct plating, and it is important to use culture-independent techniques (Jansson and Prosser 1997). Therefore, the agar plate counts to estimate the numbers of bacteria that many laboratories rely on can not estimate the number of dead or non-culturable bacterial cells.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"wbhvv","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"pv4x0","text":"So far, there are many published methods for bacterial cell quantification, including advanced culture-based methods with live/dead staining using microscopy or flow cytometry, molecular biological methods (combination of polymerase chain reaction with viability dyes) (Schottroff et al., 2018) and a microfluidic chip-based device (on-chip flow cytometry) (Sakamoto et al., 2015) or a fibre-based spectroscopic device (Ou et al., 2019) for rapid measuring of fluorescently stained bacterial cells. However, most of these methods either cannot differentiate between a single cell and a cluster, have difficulty with determining viability, or involve a human analyst counting the cells which is time-consuming and is subject to errors. Flow cytometers, while faster and more accurate, are very expensive and technically demanding (Schottroff et al., 2018).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":281,"length":5},{"style":"italic","offset":367,"length":5},{"style":"italic","offset":423,"length":5},{"style":"italic","offset":841,"length":5}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"k46op","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2f2go","text":"The QUANTOM™Microbial Cell Counter (Logos Biosciences Ltd., Korea) is a new generation instrument which can quantitatively measure total and viable microbial cells in a solution (https://logosbio.com/quantom-tx/). It is an image-based system using two different fluorescent  dyes to label and differentiate individual dead and live bacterial cells within a tight cluster or chain, and tags them for later reference so it can easily be verified later. The system can measure wide range of cell sizes between approximately 0.3 - 50 µm (FQAs in the reference). We have satisfactorily used this instrument for quantitative measurement of total and viable bacteria using Listeria monocytogenes  and Escherichia coli O157 as model organisms. We found that results using this system correlate strongly with the agar plate counts as a standard reference method for live bacterial estimation.  Also, we have used the system to count cells of Campylobacter jejuni (total and live), and heat-killed Brucella strains, for which the total count correlated with established optical density (OD) standards. The bacteria including L. monocytogenes, C jejuni and Brucella species have not been evaluated using this instrument (FAQs in the reference).The system is very helpful for experiments using samples inoculated with pre-determined numbers of target live bacteria before obtaining agar plate counting results. In addition, this system can also determine the numbers of dead bacteria. The intent of this protocol is to provide brief procedures and technical notes to stimulate further discussions and exploit this technology for future use.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":666,"length":25},{"style":"italic","offset":694,"length":16},{"style":"italic","offset":933,"length":20},{"style":"italic","offset":988,"length":8},{"style":"italic","offset":1115,"length":26},{"style":"italic","offset":1146,"length":8}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"vtquy","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

QUANTOM™ M50 Cell Counting Slides, (Cat # Q12001 for 50 Slides or Q12002 for 500 Slides).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":2}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"jrgvi","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1sr5u","text":"2. QUANTOM™ Total Cell Staining Kit (Q13501) including QUANTOM™ Total Cell Staining Dye (Q13101), QUANTOM™ Total Cell Staining Enhancer (Q13002) and QUANTOM™ Cell Loading Buffer I (Q13001).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"kraqy","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"muw5o","text":"3. QUANTOM™ Viable Cell Staining Kit (Q13502) including QUANTOM Viable Cell Dilution Buffer (Q13004), QUANTOM Viable Cell Staining Dye (Q13201), and QUANTOM Cell Loading Buffer I (Q13001).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":2}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"kyqpi","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"wjicl","text":"4. QUANTOM™ Calibration Beads (Q13102).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"nr7cs","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"piqzb","text":"5. Dimethyl Sulfoxide (Q13003).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"irpnc","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"clwbs","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"gmos7","text":"Equipment","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":9}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ujcdw","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"djp6k","text":"1.      QUANTOM Tx™ Microbial Cell Counter system (Logos Biosystems Ltd., Korea) including QUANTOM Tx ™ Microbial Cell Counter, QUANTOMTM Centrifuge, Wi-Fi dongle and USB drive.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":135,"length":2}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"x2hli","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cjiyn","text":"2.      A computer for data analysis.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"nimmm","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Preparation of total microbial cell counting

Bacteria are cultured depending on the species as below for this protocol. Listeria monocytogens in Brain and Heart Fusion (BHI) agar or broth, E. coli in nutrient agar or broth, and Campylobacter jejuni in Muller Hinton agar or broth are cultured for approximately 18 - 20 hours at 37 ºC. The strains of Brucella abortus , B. melitensis and B. suis are cultured on a serum dextrose agar (SDA) plate at 37°C with 10% CO 2 for 72 hours, followed by subculture on an SDA slant with incubation at 37 °C with 10% CO 2 for 72 hours.

Prepare microbial cell suspensions. in or sterile physiological saline. The cells from Listeria , E. coli and Campylobacter are resuspened in sterile phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH7.4, 0.01 mM) unless specified later, and the cells from the Brucella species from SDA slant are resuspended in 5 mL of sterile physiological saline. The bacteria are kept alive, or heat-killed at 80 °C for 20 – 30 minutes. The sterility is confirmed by culture. The initial approximate bacterial concentrations are determined by using either McFarland turbidity standards or optical density using a spectrometer. An optimal range between approximately 10 6 to 10 8 cells or CFU per milliliter (mL) is obtained by further dilutions in relevant buffers. Note: The QUANTOM Tx™ can count samples with concentrations in the range of 1 x 10 5 to 1 x 10 9 cells/mL. The optimal range is 2 x 10 6 to 5 x 10 8 cells/mL (FAQs in the reference). Note: Heat-killed L. monocytogenes in BHI enrichment broth, was compared with those in PBS in our studies, and there were no significant difference observed. Note: Any calculated concentration with the order of magnitude 10 5 or lower were unreliable. Our study revealed that bacterial concentrations between 10 1 to 10 5 CFU/mL showed similar counting results of 10 5 CFU/mL.
Mix 10 µL cell sample with 1 µL QUANTOM Total Cell Staining Dye and 1 µL QUANTOM Total Cell Staining Enhancer. Mix thoroughly with gentle force.
Incubate at 37°C for 30 minutes in the dark. Mix thoroughly with gentle force.
Add 8 µL QUANTOM Cell Loading Buffer I to the mixture. Mix gently to avoid creating bubbles.

Load 5.5 µL (per side) into a QUANTOM M50 Cell Counting slide. Note: based on our study, it is possible to reuse the slides after soaking the slides in disinfectant, thoroughly washing with distilled water, and air drying.
Preparation of viable microbial cell counting

The operation details are referred to QUANTOM Tx™ Centrifuge User’s Manual. The centrifugation is to immobilize and evenly distribute the cells throughout the counting chamber to ensure accurate cell counts.
Load slides vertically in the numbered slots of the QUANTOM™ Centrifuge with the sample facing the rotor. Balance rotor symmetrically with another slide in the opposite slot.

Centrifuge at 300 RCF for 10 minutes. Centrifugation force, time and other recommended parameters may need to be optimized according to cell size to distribute cells evenly along one focal plane (Centrifuge User’s manual).

The operation details are referred to QUANTOM Tx™ User’s Manual. The QUANTOM Tx™ Microbial cell Counter captures up to 10 high resolution images and counts the cells in each. The software in the system can distinguish individual cells in various arrangements such as tight clusters or in sequence to produce accurate and reliable total bacterial cell counts. The creation and editing of protocols and settings for parameters such as faster focus and declustering level which leads to a higher sensitivity to clusters are referred to in the user’s manual.
After the machine has been turned on and the initialization has completed, insert the slide face up and sample side first into the slide port.
Adjust light level as needed (normally 5 for total, and 9 for viable cell). Note that this can affect counting results.
Adjust the focus manually using the focus bar and arrows, or press Autofocus. The autofocus further reduces variability due to human error.
Select the areas using arrows under the COUNT button based on the user’s instruction.

Press COUNT. Counting times can vary, about 30 seconds for a sample with a concentration of ~1 x 10 7 cell/mL and 10 images. The results will appear on the screen automatically and the slide will be ejected from the instrument. Note: Because the counting is based on the random selection of ten spots, in order to obtain more accurate counting results, the same slide can be read more than once, and the average can be calculated.
View a graphical representation of the data in the Histogram and Gating tab.
Calculate subsequent dilutions using the dilution calculator under Dilution.
Save the data by quick save or by naming the file and manually selecting the save location.
Transfer the data to computer using a USB drive or the Wi-Fi data transfer.

Time Taken Generally speaking, twenty minutes to four hours of incubation time are required depending on the cell type. Based on our studies, 30 minutes for the total numbers of L. monocytogenes (based on 6 strains) E. coli O157 (9 strains), C. jejuni (1 strain) and Brucella species (3 strains) are satisfactory, and for the viable cells, 30 minutes for L. monocytogenes (based on 6 strains) and 30 minutes – 1.5 hours of E. coli (9 strains) and C. jejuni (1 strain) depending on the strain and staining dyes, are required.

Anticipated results The bacterial counts and calculated total numbers per mL will be displayed. Slightly variable counts between one and more reads, and replicates for the same dilutions can be observed. To be more confident with the counts, multiple reads or replicates could be conducted and the average counts can be used for more accurate results.
Load 5.5 μL (per side) into a QUANTOM M50 Cell Counting Slide or chamber.
Add 8 μL QUANTOM Cell Loading Buffer I. Mix gently so as not to create bubbles.
Incubate at 37°C for 30 minutes – 4 hours (depending on the bacterial species and strains) in the dark. Mix again (User’s Manual; and Time Taken section).

Mix 2 μL QUANTOM Viable Cell Staining Dye with 10 μL cell sample. Note: Alternative dyes, such as fluorescent calcein AM, for viable cell staining can also be used (FAQs in the reference). Our study tested calcein AM cell-permeant dye from Thermo-Fisher Scientific (Cat# C3099) with similar results as those from Logos.

Prepare a viable microbial cell suspension with an optimal range between 2x10 6 to 5x10 8 CFU/ml by diluting cell suspensions as necessary with QUANTOM Viable Cell Dilution Buffer (see note 1). NOTE 1: Stain cells after dilution or resuspension with QUANTOM Viable Cell Dilution Buffer. PBS or water will decrease labeling efficiency. Culture media or sera may have esterase activity and lead to decreased viable cell staining and high background fluorescence (User Manual). In our study, viable L. monocytogenes in Brain and Heart Fusion (BHI) enrichment broth was compared with those in PBS, there were no significant difference observed.
creator: Hongsheng Huang
